authoringProvenance.author: OpenAI GPT-5.6 Pro candidate authoring; human review required

items.prompt: A complete BFS or iterative DFS traverses every vertex and edge entry of a graph stored as adjacency lists. Each vertex is discovered once. Derive worst-case traversal time and auxiliary space, excluding the input representation and output.

feedback.reason: Each vertex is discovered once and adjacency-list scans total the stored edge entries; visited plus frontier can hold O(V) state.
feedback.details: The vertex loop or discovery events contribute O(V). Summed neighbor iteration contributes O(E) for a directed graph and O(2E) = O(E) for an undirected graph. The visited structure and queue or stack are each bounded by O(V). The fact that BFS uses FIFO and DFS uses LIFO does not change this full-traversal asymptotic cost under the same representation.

items.prompt: A traversal of a graph with V vertices uses an adjacency matrix and, for every reached vertex, scans its entire matrix row. All vertices are reached. Derive worst-case time and auxiliary space, excluding the matrix itself and output.

feedback.reason: V reached vertices each trigger a scan of V potential neighbors, while visited and frontier remain linear in V.
feedback.details: The matrix row scan pays for absent as well as present edges, producing V × V checks. The traversal's own visited/frontier state is O(V); the O(V²) matrix is excluded by the prompt as input storage. A matrix is not categorically invalid: on a dense graph E = Θ(V²), O(V²) is also O(V + E). The representation changes the cost model, not the correctness of BFS or DFS.

items.prompt: Recursive DFS traverses an adjacency-list graph whose reachable component is a simple path of V vertices. Derive worst-case time and auxiliary space, counting the recursion stack and visited state but excluding input and output.

feedback.reason: The path still scans each vertex and edge once, while recursive depth reaches V and visited also uses O(V).
feedback.details: DFS time follows the same adjacency-list accounting as other complete traversals: O(V + E). On a path, the recursive call chain cannot unwind until it reaches the final vertex, so stack depth is V. This is why a recursive implementation can overflow even though the asymptotic algorithm is linear. An explicit stack preserves O(V) worst-case auxiliary space while moving it out of the call stack.

items.prompt: BFS traverses an adjacency-list graph consisting of a source connected to V−1 leaves. Derive worst-case time and auxiliary space, excluding input and output.

feedback.reason: All vertices and edge entries are scanned once, and one BFS layer can place nearly all vertices in the queue together.
feedback.details: The star exposes the breadth-memory boundary: after expanding the center, the queue can contain V−1 leaves. That gives O(V) frontier space in addition to O(V) visited state. Time remains O(V + E). BFS is not universally faster or cheaper than DFS; it has the decisive shortest-unweighted-path property, but its frontier width can be large.

items.prompt: Connected components are counted by scanning all V vertices and starting an adjacency-list traversal whenever a vertex is unseen. Across all components, derive worst-case time and auxiliary space, excluding input and output.

feedback.reason: The outer loop adds O(V), but visited state ensures the inner traversals partition rather than repeat graph work.
feedback.details: Every vertex is considered by the outer scan once and expanded by exactly one component traversal. Every adjacency entry is scanned in that one traversal, so the total is O(V + E), not one full traversal per component. Visited plus the largest component frontier is O(V). This amortized whole-graph accounting is the key complexity invariant of component enumeration.

items.prompt: BFS marks a vertex visited only when dequeued and has no separate queued-state check. Duplicate copies are skipped when popped after the first processed copy. Which resource warning is justified for a simple adjacency-list graph?

options.text: The result can remain correct, but up to O(E) queue entries may be generated, weakening the usual O(V) frontier bound.
Time and space are always identical to discovery-time marking because both eventually visit the same vertices.
The time is necessarily O(V²) for every graph.
Converting to an adjacency matrix restores one enqueue per vertex without changing visited timing.

feedback.reason: Each examined edge can publish another copy before the target's first dequeue, so queue-entry count is no longer bounded by vertices.
feedback.details: With discovery-time marking, each vertex is enqueued once and frontier storage is O(V). With late marking and a stale-copy guard, each adjacency exposure can produce an enqueue, giving O(E) queue operations and potentially O(E) queued entries while preserving O(V + E) order in a simple guarded implementation. If duplicate copies also rescan neighbors, work can grow further. Therefore dequeue marking is not universally operationally equivalent.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_identical: Final reachability does not capture duplicate enqueue operations or the larger frontier they can create.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.must_be_quadratic: The exact penalty depends on graph structure and duplicate handling; O(V²) is not universal.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.matrix_fix: Multiple predecessors can still observe an unmarked vertex before dequeue; representation does not supply queued state.

items.prompt: A traversal visits every reachable cell of an R × C grid, generates at most four neighbors per cell, and uses a separate visited grid plus a BFS queue or DFS stack. Derive worst-case time and auxiliary space, excluding the input grid and output.

feedback.reason: There are RC states, each checks only a constant number of moves, and visited/frontier can each scale with the state count.
feedback.details: The implicit grid graph has V = RC and E = O(RC) under bounded-degree movement. Processing every state and four direction checks gives O(RC) time. A separate visited grid is O(RC), and the frontier can also contain O(RC) cells in the worst case. In-place marking can reduce the separate visited allocation, but it does not change traversal time and requires a mutation-safe contract.

items.prompt: Kahn's topological algorithm runs on a sparse directed graph stored as outgoing adjacency lists. It builds an indegree array, processes each vertex once, and decrements once per edge. Derive worst-case time and auxiliary space, excluding input adjacency and output order.

feedback.reason: Indegree construction and updates touch every vertex and edge once; indegrees plus the ready queue are linear in vertices.
feedback.details: Initialize V indegrees and scan E edges to populate them. Every vertex enters the ready queue at most once, and every directed edge triggers one decrement when its source is processed. This totals O(V + E). The indegree array and queue use O(V) auxiliary space. With an adjacency matrix, neighbor scanning may instead cost O(V²), but the dependency invariant is unchanged.

items.prompt: A multi-source BFS on an adjacency-list graph seeds k distinct sources once, then discovers each reachable vertex at most once. Derive worst-case time and auxiliary space in terms of the whole graph, excluding input and output.

feedback.reason: Seeding costs O(k), after which the shared traversal processes each discovered vertex and edge entry once rather than once per source.
feedback.details: Initialize the distinct sources, then run one queue. Because every source is marked at initialization and every other vertex is discovered once, graph processing is O(V + E); including source enumeration gives O(k + V + E), usually simplified to O(V + E) because k ≤ V. Visited, distances, and queue are O(V). Repeating a complete BFS from every source would produce the incorrect O(k(V + E)) strategy cost for this output.
